                                                                                                                                                                       Research



Reconciling links between diversity and population stability
across global plant communities
Xiaobin Pan1 , Yann Hautier1 , Jan Lepš2,3 , Shaopeng Wang4 , Kathryn E. Barry1 ,
Manuele Bazzichetto5 , Stefano Chelli6 , Jiřı́ Doležal2,7 , Nico Eisenhauer8,9 , Franz Essl10 ,
Felı́cia M. Fischer11 , Oscar Godoy12 , Daniel Gómez-Garcı́a13 , Lars Götzenberger2,7 ,
Clara Gracia14,15 , Anaclara Guido16 , Lauren M. Hallett17,18 , Susan Harrison19 , Miao He20 ,
Andrew Hector21 , Pubin Hong4 , Forest Isbell20 , George A. Kowalchuk1 , Victor Lecegui14 ,
Xiaofei Li22 , Maowei Liang23 , Frédérique Louault24 , Maria Májeková25 , Rob Marrs26 ,
Neha Mohanbabu27 , Akira S. Mori28 , Robin J. Pakeman29 , Alain Paquette30 , Begoña Peco31 ,
Josep Peñuelas32,33 , Valério D. Pillar11 , Marta Rueda34 , Wolfgang Schmidt35 , Jules Segrestin2 ,

2 Research                                                                                                                                                                 Phytologist

  Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Beijing, 100081, China; 45Department of Plant Sciences, University of California Davis, Davis, CA 95616, USA; 46School of Life and
  Environmental Sciences, Shaoxing University, Shaoxing, 312000, Zhejiang, China; 47Institute of Wetland Ecology & Clone Ecology, Taizhou University, Taizhou, 318000,
  Zhejiang, China; 48Institute of Grassland Science, Key Laboratory of Vegetation Ecology of the Ministry of Education, Songnen Grassland Ecosystem National Observation and
  Research Station, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, China; 49Key Laboratory of Grassland Resources (Inner Mongolia Agricultural University), Ministry of
  Education, Hohhot, 010021, China



                                                             Summary
  Author for correspondence:                                  Maintaining ecological stability is essential for sustaining ecosystem functions and the bene-
  Xiaobin Pan                                                fits they provide to society. Ecological theory predicts that plant diversity destabilizes local
  Email: x.pan1@uu.nl
                                                             populations, yet empirical studies report variable effects.
                                                              We hypothesize that this discrepancy arises at least in part from differences captured by dif-
  Received: 24 July 2025                                     ferent diversity (average vs cumulative richness, i.e. the mean annual richness vs the cumula-
  Accepted: 18 December 2025
                                                             tive richness across years) and stability metrics (abundance-unweighted vs weighted mean
                                                             population stability). To test this, we analyzed data from > 8000 permanent vegetation plots
  New Phytologist (2026)                                     across biomes on five continents.
  doi: 10.1111/nph.70921                                      We found a negative (i.e. destabilizing) diversity–stability relationship when using
                                                             abundance-weighted rather than unweighted measures of population stability, which are
                                                             more influenced by dominant species. Similarly, cumulative richness – capturing total species
  Key words: average richness, biodiversity,
  cumulative richness, dominance, populations,               occurrence over time and long-term turnover – reveals a stronger destabilizing effect com-
  rare species, unweighted and weighted                      pared to average annual richness.
  population stability.                                       Our findings reveal that, when specific metrics of diversity and stability are considered,
                                                             more species and potentially the associated increase in interspecific competition tend to desta-
                                                             bilize populations across natural ecosystems world-wide – particularly those of dominant
                                                             species.



                                                                                                      Such inconsistency in diversity–population stability relation-
  Introduction
                                                                                                   ships may reflect the distinct ecological processes captured by the
  The diversity–stability relationship has been the subject of long-                               metrics used to measure both plant diversity and population sta-
  standing debate, and understanding its drivers remains a central                                 bility. For example, the methodology used to estimate population
  focus in ecology (MacArthur, 1955; Goodman, 1975; Loreau &                                       stability differs between studies. Since Tilman’s (1996) seminal
  de Mazancourt, 2013; de Bello et al., 2021). Early ecological the-                               work, population stability has commonly been assessed as the
  ory suggested that complex, diverse ecosystems are inherently                                    inverse of the coefficient of variation in the abundance of indivi-
  more stable than simpler ones (Odum & Eugene, 1953;                                              dual species within a community (McGrady-Steed &
  MacArthur, 1955; Elton & Charles, 1958). Later theoretical                                       Morin, 2000; Romanuk et al., 2006; Tilman et al., 2006). To
  work, however, challenged this idea, demonstrating that diversity                                account for variations across individual species (Romanuk &
  could have a destabilizing effect (Gardner & Ashby, 1970;                                        Kolasa, 2004), population stability was later calculated by aver-
  May, 1972). Subsequent studies of plant productivity reconciled                                  aging the stability across species within a community, that is,
  these views by showing that the diversity–stability relationship                                 unweighted mean population stability (Steiner et al., 2005;
  depends on the level of organization: plant diversity enhances the                               Romanuk et al., 2009; Houlahan et al., 2018). Campbell
  temporal invariability of productivity at the community level (i.e.                              et al. (2011) found that species richness has a stabilizing effect
  community stability) but reduces it at the population levels (i.e.                               when population stability is calculated as the mean stability across
  population stability) (Tilman, 1996; Caldeira et al., 2005; Til-                                 all populations, whereas assessment within individual popula-
  man et al., 2006; Hector et al., 2010). It is now widely accepted                                tions is more likely to yield a destabilizing effect. Further theore-
  that species diversity increases community stability (Cardinale                                  tical developments have suggested that community stability is
  et al., 2012; Hautier et al., 2015; Isbell et al., 2015; Xu                                      more closely related to the weighted mean population stability, as
  et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2022), though these effects may be                                   the stability of dominant species tends to drive overall commu-
  weaker in natural vs experimental settings (Blüthgen et al., 2016;                              nity patterns (Thibaut & Connolly, 2013; Wang & Lor-
  van der Plas, 2019; Valencia et al., 2020; Lisner et al., 2024).                                 eau, 2014, 2016).
  However, considerable debate remains over whether species                                           The key difference between the unweighted and weighted
  diversity increases or decreases population stability. In particular,                            mean population stability is that the latter assigns proportionally
  a recent meta-analysis reported that population stability increases                              greater weight to dominant species (Fig. 1a). Empirical studies
  with diversity in observational studies but decreases with diversity                             have often found that dominant species tend to be more stable
  in experimental studies (Xu et al., 2021).                                                       than non-dominant ones – an effect not merely due to sampling

  New Phytologist (2026)                                                                                                                                      Ó 2026 The Author(s).
  www.newphytologist.com                                                                                                          New Phytologist Ó 2026 New Phytologist Foundation.
New
Phytologist                                                                                                                                         Research 3


artifacts but reflecting biological mechanisms, for instance the                 or transient species that are not consistently present in a commu-
buffering effect of large populations against demographic stochas-               nity but better reflect the potential pool of species that coexist
ticity (Bai et al., 2004; Roscher et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2020).              across years. As a result, some included species likely exhibit
This pattern aligns with Taylor’s power law (Taylor, 1961),                      strong fluctuating abundance over time. Higher cumulative rich-
which describes a scaling relationship between the variance and                  ness may therefore amplify population fluctuations (Til-
the mean of population abundance across time or space. Accord-                   man, 1999; Lehman & Tilman, 2000), resulting in a negative
ing to this law, species with higher mean abundance tend to have                 cumulative richness–population stability relationship (orange
proportionally lower variability when the scaling factor is < 2,                 lines in Fig. 1b,c). By contrast, average richness represents the
resulting in greater temporal stability (Segrestin et al., 2024). If             typical number of species co-occurring at a given time. It captures
this is the case, as species diversity increases, the greater inclusion          the result of community assembly due to local biotic interactions
of non-dominant, but unstable species would result in a more                     (e.g. competition and facilitation) and environmental filtering
negative diversity–population stability relationship when using                  under particular annual conditions. It is also important to stress
unweighted population stability (compare solid and dashed lines                  that the relationship between diversity and population stability
of the same color in Fig. 1b,c).                                                 can differ partly due to mathematical constraints linked to the
   How species diversity is estimated also differs across studies.               indices considered. For example, the ratio of cumulative to aver-
Some studies use ‘cumulative richness’ – the total number of spe-                age richness could represent a sort of temporal turnover. It should
cies recorded in a plot across all sampling years within a time ser-             increase with more species absences over time. Additionally, spe-
ies (Tilman et al., 2006; Hector et al., 2010; Roscher                           cies with frequent absences are typically those with lower abun-
et al., 2011). Others use ‘average richness’ – defined as the mean               dance and stability (Supporting Information Notes S1). As a
number of species observed at a location, calculated by averaging                result, higher temporal compositional turnover can be associated
annual species richness across years (Houlahan et al., 2018; Liang               with lower population stability. This pattern is likely stronger for
et al., 2025). It is important to stress that these measures likely              unweighted population stability, which is more influenced by
emphasize slightly different ecological processes (Hagan                         rare species. Therefore, the relationship between population sta-
et al., 2021; Allan et al., 2025). Cumulative richness includes rare             bility and cumulative richness is expected to be more negative, or




Fig. 1 Conceptual diagram illustrating (a) the concept of species diversity (average richness, mean annual richness; cumulative richness, cumulative
richness across years), and population stability metrics (unweighted mean, weighting species by taking them equally; weighted mean, weighting species by
their realized mean relative abundance across all years); (b) relationships between plant species diversity and population stability, considering the emergent
properties of species diversity and population stability metrics; and (c) the expected correlation coefficients (r) between plant diversity and population
stability based on the relationships in (b). Correlation coefficients are used as they are not affected by sampling size, allowing for comparisons between
datasets.

Ó 2026 The Author(s).                                                                                                                  New Phytologist (2026)
New Phytologist Ó 2026 New Phytologist Foundation.                                                                                 www.newphytologist.com
                                                                                                                                       New
4 Research                                                                                                                             Phytologist

  at least less positive, than with average richness (blue lines in      temperatures from 11.5 to 20.1°C. More details on LOTVS
  Fig. 1b,c).                                                            are available in Sperandii et al. (2022).
     Beyond differences in diversity and stability metrics, the
  strength and direction of the diversity–population stability rela-     Estimation of population stability and richness
  tionship can vary considerably among ecosystems, experiments,
  and study designs. For instance, climatic conditions and distur-       We quantified temporal stability as the invariability of popula-
  bance regimes can alter species interactions and the relative          tion abundance (Tilman et al., 2006). Specifically, we computed
  importance of dominant vs rare species (Loreau & de Mazan-             the coefficient of variation (CV), defined as the SD of population
  court, 2013). Likewise, experimental design, such as the type of       abundance divided by its temporal mean across years. To account
  abundance metric (biomass, cover, or frequency), plot size, and        for directional changes in species abundance over time, we also
  study duration, may influence how temporal stability is captured       calculated detrended population CV using the three-term local
  (Campbell et al., 2011). Understanding how these abiotic and           variance method (Lepš et al., 2019). Within each plot, species
  biotic factors jointly modulate the relationship is therefore essen-   that occurred in fewer than 30% of sampling years (e.g. species
  tial to reconcile conflicting evidence and identify general ecologi-   that appeared for fewer than 3 yr in plots sampled over a 10-yr
  cal drivers.                                                           period) were excluded from the following analysis, since measures
     In this context, we examined the empirical relationships            of temporal stability can be extremely volatile with data with
  between plant species diversity and population stability across        excessive zeros (Májeková et al., 2014; Valencia et al., 2020). We
  natural and semi-natural systems spanning diverse vegetation           also applied 10% and 20% species exclusion thresholds to
  types on five continents. Specifically, we compared two measures       demonstrate that our results are robust to the choice of threshold.
  of species diversity: (1) average richness (the average annual spe-    We calculated abundance-unweighted and weighted mean popu-
  cies richness across years) and (2) cumulative richness (the total     lation CV (hereafter UPCV and WPCV, respectively) for each
  number of species recorded in a plot across all years), alongside      plot as:
  two measures of population stability (abundance-unweighted vs
  abundance-weighted mean population stability). We expect                         1 S σi
                                                                         UPCV =       ∑
  (Fig. 1) that inconsistencies across results can be attributed at                S i = 1 μi
  least in part to different indices considered in measuring species
  richness and mean population stability, with more destabilizing                    S    σi
                                                                         WPCV = ∑             pi
  effects when considering the role of non-dominant species                         i = 1 μi
  (unweighted population stability) and the total amount of species
  found in a location (cumulative richness) compared to weighted         where S is the number of species in the plot, μi is the mean abun-
  population stability and annual richness. We further tested the        dance of species i across years, σ i is the SD of population abun-
  degree to which the diversity–population stability relationship        dance for species i, and p i is the relative abundance of species i.
  varies across abiotic and biotic gradients.                               To assess the influence of relative abundance on the diversity–
                                                                         population stability relationship, we classified species based on
                                                                         their mean abundance across all sampling years: dominant species
  Materials and Methods
                                                                         as the most abundant one within a community, rare species as
                                                                         those with a mean relative abundance of < 5%, and subordinate
  Datasets
                                                                         species as all others (Mouillot et al., 2013; Avolio et al., 2019).
  We used 88 datasets from the LOTVS collection of temporal              We then calculated unweighted mean population CV for domi-
  vegetation data (https://lotvs.csic.es). This comprehensive collec-    nant, subordinate, and rare species, respectively (hereafter
  tion comprises data for > 11 000 permanent plots of natural and        UPCV_D, UPCV_S, and UPCV_R).
  semi-natural vegetation that have been consistently sampled for           To quantify species richness over time, we calculated both
  periods ranging between 6 and 99 yr. None of the plots included        average and cumulative richness for each plot. Average richness
  in our analysis was part of a manipulated biodiversity experiment.     refers to the mean number of species observed in a plot across all
  After excluding plots with multiple sampling events within the         sampling years, while cumulative richness is the total number of
  same year, we retained 8243 plots for analysis. These data were        unique species recorded in a plot over the same period. Temporal
  collected from study sites across various vegetation types (forest     turnover was then defined using the classical multiplicative
  understory, grassland, and shrubland) on five continents (Europe,      approach as the ratio of cumulative to average richness (Whit-
  America, Africa, Asia, and Australia) (Fig. S1). These datasets        taker, 1972).
  vary in their sampling methodology (e.g. aboveground biomass,
  visual species cover, and species frequency), plot size, duration of
                                                                         Data analysis
  sampling, and management regimes (e.g. grazing, fertilization,
  and burning), which allows for more generalizable conclusions.         We first performed Pearson correlation tests for each dataset to
  Climatic conditions also vary widely, with mean annual precipi-        estimate relationships between species diversity (average and
  tation ranging from 140 to 2592 mm and mean annual                     cumulative richness) and population stability (UPCV and


  New Phytologist (2026)                                                                                                        Ó 2026 The Author(s).
  www.newphytologist.com                                                                            New Phytologist Ó 2026 New Phytologist Foundation.
New
Phytologist                                                                                                                           Research 5


WPCV) across plots. Correlation tests were used because the cor-          LME4 (Bates et al., 2015), METAFOR (Viechtbauer, 2010), PATCH-
relation coefficient (r) is unaffected by sample size, enabling           WORK (Pedersen, 2024), TIDYVERSE (Wickham et al., 2019), and
meaningful comparisons between datasets. All parameters were              VEGAN (Oksanen et al., 2024).
log-transformed to improve normality. To ensure reliability, we
excluded datasets with fewer than five plots, leaving 84 datasets.
                                                                          Results
One additional dataset was removed for cumulative richness
because it showed no variation across plots. For each combina-            The relationship between plant diversity and population stability
tion of diversity and population stability metrics, we calculated         strongly depended on the choice of diversity and population sta-
the correlation coefficient (r) for each dataset. To ensure consis-       bility metrics. A linear mixed-effects model confirmed this, iden-
tency in interpretation, we reversed the sign of r values so that         tifying an interaction between diversity and population stability
positive r values always indicate a positive relationship between         metrics on the correlation coefficient (r) (F1,246.4 = 12.73,
diversity and stability (Xu et al., 2021).                                P < 0.001; Table S1). The relationship between average richness
   To analyze whether the diversity–population stability relation-        and unweighted population stability was positive (r = 0.09,
ship depends on the metrics used, we utilized (1) linear mixed-           P = 0.026). Fifty-three of the 84 datasets showed a positive rela-
effects models with r values as response variable, metrics of species     tionship, with 21 being significant (Fig. 2a). By contrast, the rela-
diversity, of population stability, and their interaction as explana-     tionship between average richness and weighted population
tory variables, and dataset as a random factor, and (2) random-           stability was, on average, negative (r = 0.08, P = 0.030;
effects meta-regression models fitted with restricted maximum like-       Fig. 2b). We observed predominantly negative relationships
lihood for each metric combination. For the meta-regression mod-          between cumulative richness and population stability (Fig. 2c,d),
els, effect sizes were Fisher’s Z-transformed correlation coefficients,   with a weaker relationship for unweighted (r =                  0.18,
with sampling variances calculated based on the number of plots           P < 0.001) than for weighted population stability (r = 0.21,
per dataset. As both approaches produced similar results, we report       P < 0.001). Results remained consistent when using detrended
only the outcomes from the linear mixed-effects models in the             population CV (Table S2) and when applying 10 and 20% exclu-
main text. We applied the same approach to examine the relation-          sion thresholds (Tables S3, S4). Within datasets, changes in r
ships between species diversity (average and cumulative richness)         values from average to cumulative richness were consistently
and the temporal stability of dominant, subordinate, and rare spe-        negative for unweighted population stability (Fig. S2A) and
cies (UPCV_D, UPCV_S, and UPCV_R), and extracted the corre-               mostly negative for weighted population stability (Fig. S2B),
sponding r values. We then tested whether these r values were             meaning that within-site relationships became consistently more
associated with those from the correlations between species diversity     negative when using cumulative richness. However, the change
and overall population stability (UPCV and WPCV). We also                 from unweighted to weighted population stability was generally
tested the correlation between temporal turnover and population           negative for average richness (Fig. S2C) but not for cumulative
stability (UPCV and WPCV). To further explore dynamics among              richness (Fig. S2D).
different groups, we calculated the mean population size (i.e. mean          Our results revealed that the diversity–population stability
abundance across years) for dominant, subordinate, and rare species       relationship varied among dominant, subordinate, and rare spe-
in each plot and assessed how their mean population size responded        cies (Table S5). The average correlation between average richness
to increasing diversity.                                                  and population stability of rare species was positive (r = 0.25,
   Finally, we evaluated how the strength of the diversity–               P < 0.001). Sixty-seven of the 82 datasets were positive, with 31
population stability relationship varied among the datasets. We           of these being significant (Fig. 3a). Similarly, average richness
ran linear models for each combination of diversity and popula-           was positively correlated with population stability of subordinate
tion stability metrics to test the effects of abiotic and biotic vari-    species (r = 0.15, P < 0.001; Fig. 3b). However, the number of
ables on r values. The explanatory variables included type of             positive and negative relationships between average richness and
abundance metric (biomass, frequency, or cover), plot size (m2),          population stability of dominant species were similar across data-
study duration (sampling years), management coverage (percen-             sets (Fig. 3c). Using cumulative richness increased the number of
tage of control plot within the dataset), mean average richness,          negative relationships, leading to comparable amounts of positive
and two climate axes derived from principal component analysis            and negative relationships for the population stability of rare
(data from Gracia et al., 2026, 71.6% of the total variance was           (Fig. 3d) and subordinate species (Fig. 3e) but a marginally sig-
explained by the first two axes): climate PC1 (dryness and cold-          nificantly negative relationship for the population stability of
ness) and climate PC2 (extreme climate events). We used cover             dominant species (r = 0.06, P = 0.086; Fig. 3f). The r values
as the reference level for the abundance metric, and                      for diversity vs unweighted population stability were strongly cor-
log-transformed plot size and study duration to correct for skew-         related with those for diversity vs population stability of rare spe-
ness. Variance inflation factors for all abiotic and biotic predic-       cies (R2 = 0.73, P < 0.001; Fig. S3A). By contrast, the r values
tors were below 2, indicating no problematic multicollinearity            for diversity vs weighted population stability were strongly corre-
among these variables.                                                    lated with those for diversity vs population stability of dominant
   The analyses and graphical outputs were performed using R              species (R2 = 0.62, P < 0.001; Fig. S3F).
software v.4.4.1(R Core Team, 2024), with the following                      Our results showed a strong negative mean correlation
packages: EMMEANS (Lenth, 2024), GGPLOT2 (Wickham, 2016),                 between temporal turnover and unweighted population stability

                                                                                                (r) for each dataset between average richness
                                                                                                and unweighted population stability (a),
                                                                                                average richness and weighted population
                                                                                                stability (b), cumulative richness and
                                                n
  n




                                                                                                unweighted population stability (c), and
                                                                                                cumulative richness and weighted population
                                                                                                stability (d). Different numbers of datasets were
                                                                                                used due to the selection process. Significant
                                                                                                positive, significant negative, and nonsignificant
                                                                                                correlations are represented by blue, orange,
                                                                                                and gray colors, respectively. Mean correlation
                                                                                                coefficients (r) and 95% confidence intervals
                                                                                                from linear mixed-effects models are also shown
                                                                                                within each panel.


  (r = 0.76, P < 0.001; Fig. 4a; Table S6) but a weaker mean             of diversity and population stability metrics. We used observa-
  correlation with weighted population stability (r = 0.36,              tional data because, in biodiversity experiments, the high correla-
  P < 0.001; Fig. 4b; Table S6). Analysis of mean population size        tion between average and cumulative richness due to weeding
  against diversity showed different patterns for dominant, subor-       can obscure these relationships. While we observed a generally
  dinate, and rare species (Table S7). Mean population size of rare      positive (i.e. stabilizing) relationship between average richness
  species increased with average richness (r = 0.18, P < 0.001;          and unweighted population stability (metrics commonly used in
  Fig. 5a), but not with cumulative richness (Fig. 5d). For subordi-     field studies), our findings revealed increasingly negative (i.e.
  nate species, we found no change in population size with either        destabilizing) relationships when richness was accumulated across
  average (Fig. 5b) or cumulative richness (Fig. 5e). For dominant       years and when stability metrics accounted for species domi-
  species, however, mean population size decreased with both aver-       nance. This suggests that changing species composition as a result
  age (r = 0.09, P = 0.039; Fig. 5c) and cumulative richness (r          of both deterministic and stochastic processes causes less stability
  = 0.13, P = 0.003; Fig. 5f). Our results also indicated that the       in diverse plant communities.
  relationships between species diversity and population stability
  were consistent (Fig. 6). Among the tested variables, only mean
                                                                         Average vs cumulative richness
  average richness significantly drove the relationship between
  diversity and unweighted population stability to be more posi-         The relationships between plant diversity and population stability
  tive. This positive effect indicated that the link between diversity   differed with the use of average vs cumulative richness. Consis-
  and unweighted population stability was stronger at sites with         tent with our hypotheses, cumulative richness was negatively cor-
  more species.                                                          related with population stability, while average richness was more
                                                                         often positively correlated. These findings are supported by
                                                                         observational studies reporting positive associations between
  Discussion
                                                                         average richness and population stability (Houlahan et al., 2018;
  Our study reconciles links between plant diversity and popula-         Xu et al., 2021), as well as by numerous experimental studies
  tion stability by revealing that inconsistencies in the relationship   showing that increased cumulative richness reduces population
  across studies stem, at least in part, from the emerging properties    stability (Tilman et al., 2006; Hector et al., 2010; Roscher

  New Phytologist (2026)                                                                                                    Ó 2026 The Author(s).
  www.newphytologist.com                                                                        New Phytologist Ó 2026 New Phytologist Foundation.

Phytologist                                                                                                                                          Research 7




Fig. 3 Histograms of the correlation coefficients (r) for each dataset between average richness and mean population stability of rare species (a),
subordinate species (b), and dominant species (c), and between cumulative richness and mean population stability of rare species (d), subordinate species
(e), and dominant species (f). All the variables were ln-transformed. Different numbers of datasets were used due to the selection process. Significant
positive, significant negative, and nonsignificant correlations are represented by blue, orange, and gray colors, respectively. Mean correlation coefficients
(r) and 95% confidence intervals from linear mixed-effects models are also shown within each panel.



                                                         (a)                                               (b)




Fig. 4 Histograms of the correlation coefficients
(r) for each dataset between temporal turnover
                                                                                                           n
                                                      n




and unweighted population stability (a), and
weighted population stability (b). All the
variables were ln-transformed. Significant
positive, significant negative, and
nonsignificant correlations are represented by
blue, orange, and gray colors, respectively.
Mean correlation coefficients (r) and 95%
confidence intervals from linear mixed-effects
models are also shown within each panel.



et al., 2011). Our results suggest that the differing relationships                  Cumulative richness, which reflects the broader species pool at
observed with average vs cumulative richness may arise from (1)                   a location over time, plays a key role in shaping species interac-
the dominance of different ecological mechanisms and (2) math-                    tion dynamics. This long-term measure provides valuable
ematical necessity.                                                               insights into how competition develops across years (Cornell &

8 Research                                                                                                                                    Phytologist

       (d)                                            (e)                                                (f)
                                                       n




  Fig. 5 Histograms of the correlation coefficients (r) for each dataset between average richness and mean population size of rare species (a), subordinate
  species (b), and dominant species (c), and between cumulative richness and mean population size of rare species (d), subordinate species (e), and dominant
  species (f). All the variables were ln-transformed. Mean population size was scaled within each data type. Different numbers of datasets were used due to
  the selection process. Significant positive, significant negative, and nonsignificant correlations are represented by blue, orange, and gray colors,
  respectively. Mean correlation coefficients (r) and 95% confidence intervals from linear mixed-effects models are also shown within each panel.


  Harrison, 2014). Interspecific competition, where dominant spe-                negative relationship between temporal turnover and unweighted
  cies exclude others by monopolizing space and resources (Til-                  population stability. This mechanism requires rare species to be
  man, 1982), can increase the variance of population abundance                  less stable, a pattern consistent with Taylor’s power law (Tay-
  across years, thereby reducing overall population stability. As                lor, 1961), which posits that dominant species have higher stabi-
  cumulative richness is more likely to include transient and rare               lity when the scaling factor is < 2. A recent study using the same
  species that exhibit strong temporal fluctuations over time, this              datasets revealed a widespread scaling effect consistent with Tay-
  will result in a negative relationship between cumulative richness             lor’s power law, with scaling factors predominantly < 2 (Gracia
  and population stability. Average richness, on the other hand,                 et al., 2026), further supporting our results.
  reflects typical species composition and abundance at a location
  over time. Theoretical models predict that mean population
                                                                                 Unweighted vs weighted population stability
  abundance decreases with diversity if total community abun-
  dance remains constant (Tilman, 1999), yet natural communities                 Contrary to our hypotheses, we found a more positive relation-
  often deviate from this pattern, with community diversity and                  ship between diversity and population stability when using
  population abundances positively covarying (Kaspari et al., 2000;              unweighted population stability. This result was unexpected, as
  Valone & Hoffman, 2003). In line with this point, our results                  dominant species are expected to be more stable than
  showed that the population size of rare species increased with                 non-dominant species, based on Taylor’s power law (Tay-
  average richness. This pattern may be explained by strong facili-              lor, 1961). However, further analyses revealed that the relation-
  tation or niche complementarity among rare species in diverse                  ship differs among dominant and rare species, providing a
  communities.                                                                   compelling explanation for this unexpected pattern. Specifically,
     Our results also confirmed that the differing relationships stem            the diversity–unweighted population stability relationship was
  partially from mathematical necessity, as evidenced by the strong,             primarily explained by rare species (r2 = 0.73; Fig. S3A), whose

  New Phytologist (2026)                                                                                                               Ó 2026 The Author(s).
  www.newphytologist.com                                                                                   New Phytologist Ó 2026 New Phytologist Foundation.

Phytologist                                                                                                                       Research 9


                                                     (a)                                         (b)




                                                     (c)                                         (d)



Fig. 6 Influence of multiple abiotic and biotic
drivers on the correlation coefficient (r)
between: (a) average richness and unweighted
population stability, (b) cumulative richness and
unweighted population stability, (c) average
richness and weighted population stability, and
(d) cumulative richness and weighted population
stability. Colors represent different driver
categories: type of abundance metric (purple;
cover as the reference level), plot attributes
(blue), biotic attributes (orange), and climatic
variables (gray). Estimated coefficients and their
95% confidence intervals are shown; significant
effects (P < 0.05) are indicated by *.



population stability increased with average richness, while the       twofold greater in grasslands (Estimate = 0.199) than in forests
diversity–weighted population stability relationship was driven       (Estimate = 0.110). However, this difference was not statistically
by dominant species (r2 = 0.62; Fig. S3F), for which population       significant (F1,61 = 0.79, P = 0.379), likely due to the lower num-
stability negatively correlated with cumulative richness. This sug-   ber of forest datasets (n = 6) compared with grassland datasets
gests that in richer communities, rare species become more stable,    (n = 57). Future research could further evaluate this pattern using
whereas dominant species tend to deflate. These differences were      broader and more comprehensive datasets. Consequently, the rela-
partly driven by variations in population size: the population size   tionship between diversity and population stability became more
of rare species increased with average richness, whereas the popu-    positive when using unweighted population stability. Our study
lation size of dominant species decreased with both average and       demonstrates, for the first time, that the relationship between
cumulative richness.                                                  diversity and population stability differs significantly between
   One potential mechanism underlying these results is plant–         dominant and rare species, providing new insights into the
fungal mutualism, particularly associations with arbuscular mycor-    mechanisms underlying diversity–stability relationships.
rhizal fungi (AMF). AMF are known to reduce fitness differences
among coexisting plant species by enhancing nutrient and water
                                                                      Drivers of the diversity–population stability relationship
uptake, thereby promoting coexistence (Willing et al., 2024).
Under this scenario, rare species gain better access to resources,    Previous research suggests that different measures of abundance
leading to higher mean abundance and greater stability. Conver-       (e.g. cover or biomass) can influence population stability (Pan
sely, dominant species may experience a dilution of their competi-    et al., 2024), thereby influencing the diversity–population stability
tive advantage, resulting in lower mean abundance and reduced         relationship. Cottingham et al. (2001) also suggested that diversity–
stability. This mechanism is expected to operate more strongly in     stability relationships are mediated by factors varying across com-
AMF-dominated grasslands than in ectomycorrhizal (ECM)-               munity types. By contrast, our results showed that these relation-
dominated forests. Supporting this interpretation, we found           ships remained consistent across abiotic and biotic gradients. We
that the change in correlation coefficient from unweighted to         found that only mean average richness positively influenced the
weighted population stability for average richness was nearly         diversity–unweighted population stability relationship, with sites of

Ó 2026 The Author(s).                                                                                                 New Phytologist (2026)
New Phytologist Ó 2026 New Phytologist Foundation.                                                                www.newphytologist.com
                                                                                                                                     New
10 Research                                                                                                                          Phytologist

   higher richness tending to support more rare species, thereby           authors contributed critically to the drafts and gave final approval
   enhancing stability. This effect was nonsignificant for weighted        for publication.
   population stability, which was more influenced by dominant spe-
   cies. This finding again supports our previous results that rare spe-
                                                                           ORCID
   cies tend to have positive diversity–stability relationships.
      In line with previous research (Houlahan et al., 2018), our          Kathryn E. Barry https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6893-6479
   results confirm that, even after accounting for different diversity     Manuele Bazzichetto https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9874-5064
   and stability metrics, substantial variation remains in the strength    Francesco de Bello https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9202-8198
   and direction of diversity–population stability relationships. This     Stefano Chelli https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7184-8242
   residual variation likely reflects additional ecological influences     Jiřı́ Doležal https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5829-4051
   not accounted for in our models, such as variation in soil proper-      Nico Eisenhauer https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0371-6720
   ties and AMF. These findings underscore the need for integrative        Franz Essl https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8253-2112
   approaches that combine richness metrics with ecological con-           Felı́cia M. Fischer https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2319-3648
   text. Future research should identify the ecological and environ-       Daniel Gómez-Garcı́a https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9738-
   mental conditions under which diversity–stability relationships         8720
   become more consistent and predictable across ecosystems.               Lars Götzenberger https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3040-2900
                                                                           Oscar Godoy https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4988-6626
                                                                           Clara Gracia https://orcid.org/0009-0001-3855-4012
   Conclusions
                                                                           Anaclara Guido https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2453-048X
   To our knowledge, our study is the first to explore the joint           Lauren M. Hallett https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0718-0257
   effects of the emerging properties of different species diversity       Susan Harrison https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6278-2181
   and population stability metrics on the diversity–population sta-       Yann Hautier https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4347-7741
   bility relationship in natural communities. We thereby demon-           Miao He https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1851-9797
   strate that the inconsistencies in previous findings can be             Andrew Hector https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1309-7716
   partially reconciled by accounting for the influence of different       Pubin Hong https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8728-5464
   metrics. These findings offer valuable insights into the longstand-     Forest Isbell https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9689-769X
   ing diversity–stability debate and have important implications          George A. Kowalchuk https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3866-
   for conservation strategies.                                            0832
                                                                           Victor Lecegui https://orcid.org/0009-0001-6839-0709
                                                                           Jan Lepš https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4822-7429

   Viechtbauer W. 2010. Conducting meta-analyses in R with the METAFOR package.
     Journal of Statistical Software 36: 1–48.                                              the correlation coefficient (r) with the 20% threshold method.
